items.title: The server was healthy. The monitor was asking the wrong door.
items.hook: A production alert kept reporting SSH failure even while the website served traffic normally.
items.goal: Find why every health cycle marked the domestic control server as unreachable.
items.outcome: The audit rule was corrected from retired port 22 to management port 443, ending the false alerts.

events.title: Web checks stayed green
events.summary: Public API checks returned 200 while the SSH monitor failed.

events.title: Repeated TCP timeout
events.summary: The checker attempted 140.143.142.51:22, a port no longer used for management.

events.title: Treat this as a monitor defect
events.summary: Because the public service was healthy and port 443 was the documented management entry, the alert rule was stale rather than the server being down.

events.title: Replace the fixed port
events.summary: All recurring audits were updated to use the shared SSH entry on port 443.

events.title: Next cycle completed
events.summary: The checker reached systemd and logs without producing the old false positive.

events.title: Signal restored
events.summary: Future alerts now distinguish an unavailable service from an obsolete check path.

items.title: No trades for days, but the strategy was not the only problem.
items.hook: Loosening the entry threshold changed nothing because a rejected order path looked like a quiet market.
items.goal: Separate overly strict strategy conditions from a broken execution path.
items.outcome: Order rejection became observable, risk checks remained intact, and paper trades could finally validate the strategy.

events.title: Signals existed without positions
events.summary: The monitor saw qualifying market states, yet no open order or position followed.

events.title: Execution errors were swallowed
events.summary: The exchange response failed validation and was reduced to a generic no-trade result.

events.title: Fix observability before tuning
events.summary: Relaxing conditions again would hide the execution defect, so order-state reporting was repaired first.

events.title: Preserve rejection details
events.summary: The bot now records exchange code, risk gate, requested size, and final order state without secrets.

events.title: Paper order passed
events.summary: A bounded test order entered the expected simulated position and appeared in the audit snapshot.

events.title: Strategy testing resumed
events.summary: The system can now tell a weak signal from a failed order instead of reporting both as silence.

items.title: A growth page hid the product it was supposed to sell.
items.hook: Visitors landed on registration guidance before they could see a single piece of real network content.
items.goal: Restore exploration without discarding the existing agent network.
items.outcome: Public content moved above onboarding, the broken agent loader was repaired, and registration became a secondary action.

events.title: First viewport had no content
events.summary: The dominant panel asked visitors to connect an agent before proving why the network mattered.

events.title: Featured agents never loaded
events.summary: The rendering function existed but was not invoked during page startup.

events.title: Let visitors explore first
events.summary: Acquisition copy could not compensate for an empty experience, so real content became the first interaction.

events.title: Reorder and repair
events.summary: The content stage moved ahead of onboarding and the external-agent loader was wired into initialization.

events.title: Test and production matched
events.summary: Desktop and mobile checks showed public content before the connection call to action.

events.title: The product became visible
events.summary: New visitors could inspect the network before deciding whether to connect an agent.
